text: The surgical setup includes a cadier forceps in the surgeon's right hand for retraction, a vessel sealer as the active instrument on the right side, and a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand. A Nathanson retractor is positioned to elevate the left lobe of the liver.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer and fenestrated bipolar to separate the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, carefully preserving the gastroepiploic vessels. The instruments move steadily from the body towards the gastroesophageal junction, encountering and managing the short gastric vessels to ensure precise hemostasis and prevent bleeding.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully detach the last portion of the greater omentum from the greater curvature of the stomach, ensuring the entire curvature is free from attachments. The retrogastric tissues are addressed, with the vessel sealer efficiently handling the flimsy adhesions. The surgeon prepares for stapling by confirming the complete release of the fundus and gastroesophageal junction from the splenic pole attachments, then shifts focus towards the lower stomach near the pylorus, aiming for separation approximately five centimeters from the pylorus.

The surgeon flexes the vessel sealer to perform precise hemostatic maneuvers, removing the omentum from the surgical field near the greater curvature of the stomach, approximately five centimeters from the pyloric region. A robotic stapler is introduced, and a bougie is in place to guide the creation of a stomach sleeve. The stapler articulates to ensure precise apposition of the anterior and posterior stomach walls, with indicators showing the stapling process in progress.

The surgeon uses robotic staplers to progressively fire along the natural curvature of the stomach, flexing the staplers towards the left side of the screen to create a precise sleeve gastrectomy. The bougie is in place to prevent narrowing, and the stapler line is adjusted to ensure proper division of the fundus near the gastroesophageal junction. The final firing completes the sleeve gastrectomy, with the stapler tilting leftward to align with the anatomical markers.
The surgeon performs a leak test by instilling 100 to 120 mL of fluid into the newly created sleeve gastrectomy to ensure the anastomosis is waterproof and leak-proof.
